critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experiment design with thoughtful controls. I'll analyze the genuine methodological issues rather than manufacture concerns.

**Issue 1: Preprocessing leakage via full-corpus normalization**

The design explicitly states preprocessing transformers are 'parameterized once using the retained 18-month telemetry corpus' before model fitting. This means test-set statistics (months 14–18) inform normalization parameters, scaling, and imputation defaults used at training time. For mean/variance normalization of heavy-tailed flow features, this leaks test-set distributional information backward in time. The claim is that this 'stabilizes' heavy-tailed features; the mechanism of failure is that the normalizer will have seen the marginal distribution of future attack campaigns, which could — in theory — shift standardization in ways that make future anomalies appear less extreme than they would in a truly prospective deployment. More concretely, if months 14–18 contain a novel attack campaign that generates unusually large packets or unusual timing, the scaler has already incorporated that variance, potentially reducing the effective signal. In real deployment, the scaler would be fit only on past data. Evidence either way: refit the preprocessor using only months 1–10 and compare pAUC-ROC on the test set. If results diverge meaningfully, leakage is real. If not, the concern is moot in practice, though the design still misrepresents prospective deployment.

**Issue 2: Asymmetric feature access between the transformer and baseline is partially controlled but creates an ambiguous comparison**

The transformer uses packet-level sequences AND session metadata AND host identifiers. The baseline uses aggregated flow statistics only — no host identifiers, and critically, no DNS logs, proxy logs, or endpoint alert signals that appear in the data description. Section 5 lists 'historical features (e.g., alert count on source host in past 7 days)' in the shared metadata, but only lists these for the transformer. It is unclear whether these same historical features are available to the baseline. If the baseline is denied time-series host-level alert counts while the transformer receives them, any performance difference could be attributable to the historical alert signals rather than packet-level sequence modeling. The design's own logic in Section 6 (removing host identifiers as a confound check) does not address this: a transformer with no host IDs but with 7-day alert counts still has a form of identity information. Evidence: explicitly list every feature available to each model in a feature table and confirm the baseline has access to all non-sequence, non-identifier features in the session metadata block.

**Issue 3: 5-fold temporal CV within a 3-month validation window is geometrically problematic**

Section 7 describes '5-fold temporal cross-validation within the validation set (months 11–13).' Three months of data divided into 5 sequential folds yields folds of approximately 18 days each. The description says 'each fold is evaluated on the 4 preceding folds' hyperparameters' — this is either a description of a walk-forward scheme or is internally inconsistent (fold 1 cannot have 4 preceding folds). Setting aside the ambiguity, 18-day evaluation windows in intrusion detection are problematic because: (a) APT campaigns can span weeks, meaning a single campaign could appear in both training and evaluation portions of the same CV split, partially reintroducing temporal leakage; (b) the model sees very few distinct incidents per fold (APTs are rare), making pAUC-ROC estimates within each fold extremely high-variance; and (c) the hyperparameter selected may be tuned to idiosyncrasies of a specific short window rather than to generalizable signal. Evidence: report the incident count per fold. If any fold has fewer than ~20 confirmed incidents, the pAUC-ROC estimated there is too noisy to guide hyperparameter selection reliably.

**Issue 4: The host-identifier ablation does not isolate the sequence modeling hypothesis**

The design's primary confound check is: train a transformer without host IDs and see if performance degrades. But the remaining features still include packet-level sequences, which the baseline does not have. If the transformer-without-IDs outperforms the baseline, the design attributes this to 'sequence modeling' — but the difference could also be explained by joint modeling of sequences with other session metadata features that the transformer's attention mechanism can exploit and XGBoost cannot (e.g., cross-feature interactions between TLS handshake markers and inter-packet timing). The baseline is structurally prevented from modeling temporal dependencies within a session by design. The experiment therefore conflates three things: (1) the value of packet-level sequence features as input, (2) the value of the transformer architecture for joint feature interactions, and (3) the value of sequential/temporal inductive bias. To isolate sequence modeling specifically, a third ablation is needed: a gradient-boosted model trained on the same packet-level aggregate statistics (mean, std, skew, percentiles of the raw sequences) but without the sequential structure. If this GBM matches the transformer, the sequence modeling hypothesis is not supported — the raw feature content, not the sequence architecture, is doing the work. This is a missing baseline, not an edge case.

**Issue 5: Stratified bootstrap resampling on a temporally structured test set produces anti-conservative confidence intervals**

Section 8 uses '1000-iteration stratified bootstrap resampling' for confidence intervals on pAUC-ROC. Bootstrap resampling treats test observations as i.i.d. and resamples with replacement. In network intrusion data, sessions from the same attack campaign are not independent: they share hosts, timing, protocol fingerprints, and behavioral signatures. Resampling individual sessions will systematically underestimate the variance of the pAUC-ROC estimator because it does not capture campaign-level clustering. The confidence intervals will be anti-conservative (too narrow), making it easier to declare statistical significance when the true uncertainty is larger. The correct approach is block bootstrap (blocking by campaign or by time window) or a permutation test that respects temporal structure. Evidence: compare block bootstrap CIs (blocking by confirmed incident or by week) to session-level bootstrap CIs. If the block CIs are substantially wider and the significance threshold is no longer met, the statistical claim is invalid.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing fitted on the full 18-month corpus including test data leaks future distributional information into normalization parameters, misrepresenting truly prospective deployment.

It is ambiguous whether the baseline receives all non-sequence session metadata features (including 7-day historical alert counts), making feature access asymmetry a confound that the host-identifier ablation does not resolve.
Five temporal folds within a 3-month window produces ~18-day evaluation windows with likely too few incidents per fold for reliable pAUC-ROC-based hyperparameter selection.
The ablation study cannot isolate sequence modeling from raw feature content because no gradient-boosted baseline trained on aggregate statistics of the raw packet sequences is included.
Session-level stratified bootstrap underestimates confidence interval width due to campaign-level clustering in network intrusion data, producing anti-conservative significance tests.
